item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is mostly how I feel about it. Some other things that struck me were that the contention that the Foundation contains it because 'a threat to capitalism is a threat to normalcy' struck me as absurd. If the Foundation existed in a socialist world, this SCP wouldn't have a reason to really do what it does because socialism would be the norm, but they'd still have to contain this particular SCP because it's still a clear anomaly. The socialist Foundation would also have to contain a hypothetical "Ms. Liberty" from doing the same thing as Ms. Zapatista but for democracy and liberalism, because Ms. Liberty would be abnormal as well. The Foundation are being framed as the bad guys for preventing an anomaly from forcibly remaking the world, something they do for a thousand other SCPs. There's also the depiction of this SCP not being explicitly pro-communist, but merely "inspiring" people it affects - the fact that they ALL become communists just being some sort of weird coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite a fasincating and enjoyable SCP and it's a reminder on a manner of modus operandi for the Foundation. Their devotion to "status quo" at least in terms of blocking anamolous phenomena is what drives them, but they also seem to lose touch with humanity sometimes.

La Dama Zapatista is a very good orator and just basically points out problems that you can no longer ignore or passively shut out. Can no longer turn away from the problems.

Which goes in contrast to the Foundation. Their entire point is hiding unpleasant stuff away. It's a fascinating second layer.

+1

Not sure where you get the idea that the foundation isn't trying to contain SCP-4669. It's a Keter-class anomaly, so they're doing what they can to minimize its impact. Take a look at Cousin Johnny for this kind of containment procedure.

Also, not everybody would be clearly affected by this scip. If that wasn't the case, then they wouldn't bother staffing MTF U-58 with "… individuals who score under a -65 on the Cohen-Weinberg compassion scale1 and [who] maintain strict anti-collectivist morals and values."

My understanding of that is when someone is a rugged individualist and capitalist at heart, they wouldn't be affected. Presumably, this can only turn someone who occasionally shares Bernie Sanders memes on Facebook into hardcore leftist "antifa thug" types. Maybe liberals, too, idk for sure tho.

Also, the Foundation lies to its employees all the time (and for good reasons). That's like a major reason for the classification levels.

Footnotes
1. An objective measurement of a human's ability to empathize and act on the suffering of other humans created by Drs. Cohen and Weinberg in containment of various objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There seems to be a bit too much pro-socialism bias here, especially with how you literally need to have low empathy to resist what's implied to be non-anomalous reasoning. Most capitalists would agree with Zapatista on the problems that the system causes, but they'll disagree on communist revolution being the cure.

Plus, there's the problem where capitalism isn't even the root of these problems. Children starving and sick people dying while the rich drown in overabundant luxury has been the status quo everywhere ever since our ancestors evolved into humans. This happened even in communist countries whose revolutionary founders carried dreams of equality and hopes of a better world. I guess real communism will finally be achieved this time?

Rant over. It's still a +1 for good writing overall, but there's some presentation of personal ideology that tickles my butthole the wrong way.

Edits were made for clarity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea that the foundation shouldn't contain her from talking is incredibly stupid. They contain a literal pizza box that makes you your favorite pizza, this is completely ridiculous, especially with the implication that anyone would go to communism if somebody told the right words, or the implication the the foundation is lying to its subordinate members to try and convince them to contain the anomaly. If an anomaly tells me to do my damn laundry and won't let me forget that's still anomalous!

Edit: changed "it's lower down members" to "its subordinate members"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i was enjoying this until footnote 5, when i began enjoying it more than any other scp i've ever read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinch hasn't permanently left the site and, from my convos with her, she doesn't plan to permanently leave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author will not be here more. She/They left the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not strictly stated that everyone is affected by 4669-1, but it's not clear either. In particular, this line:

gave me, for one, the wrong impression. Consider rewriting to clarify?

Also, aleakinetic? hazardmanipulator, dicemanipulator, gamblingmanipulator? jarring; there are better terms.

I'd be interested in discussing the political part of this in PMs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is phenomenal and jude being like "sike" after the breakout made me laugh out loud. i'm glad to see gaw following their ideology to the morally inevitable conclusion. A+, +1

item.title: Is this another 2721 waiting to happen?
item.content:encoded:
People get WAY too stirred up over nothing.
A fictional article is not going to make the proletariat take up in arms, guys.

I like this. I think it is a interesting character study, the dialogue is pretty good.

I don't necessarily agree with the "everything is political" mind set. Wouldn't know how to explain it properly, since english isn't my first languace. But, I mean, yeah, everything, from art to your preferences on clothing style, is based on some some beliefs you have. And in that sense, "everything is political", for everything we do rests on a set of subjective assumptions, which on a broader scale constitutes your ideals, which on a even broader scale constitutes your political beliefs. That said, your works and preferences may not be a reflection of your political beliefs, necessarily. Those are just part of the "belief" spectrum. And more importantly, to come out and just expose your political beliefs all out in your work, is lazy. And insulting to your audience/readership.

The reason I talk about it, is because I DON'T think this article insults me, as a reader. I think it is actually more subtle than most. There's some ambiguity about the way the message is presented(even if, in the end, the message is clear. But that's really more about the author presenting themselves as a marxist). So yeah, liked it.

Also, I'd like to board in the passive agressive train. This article is quite comical.
A socialist revolution that doesn't kill millions of poor people, and where the rich and powerful don't get more powerfull, is a pretty funny joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Capitalism is the most morally bankrupt, murderous ideology in history.

I'd say fascism occupies this slot, but that's neither here nor there (there's a complex political discussion to be had on this subject, and this is likely not the appropriate place for it).

That being said: Anyone who perceives communism (as described by Karl Marx) as a 'morally bankrupt, murderous ideology' is, well — probably not someone who knows very much about communism. Certainly not someone who has read anything substantial about it.

People conflate communism in practice (like Stalinism) with communism in theory (which is what this article is about). This is akin to judging the contents of the Bible not by what's in it, but by what actions some of its readers take (e.g., "The Crusades demonstrate that the Bible is the most morally bankrupt, murderous ideology in history").

You can't assign morality to a text based on the actions of those who read it. You can talk about the consequences of the text, but there is nothing in the Communist Manifesto that advises us to starve the Ukraine. That shit is on Stalin — not Marx.


ETA: Apologies, wrote/posted this before I saw a staff closed-post order.

item.title: Mod Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Neither of you get to call economic systems murderous. We'll stop this reply part of the thread here before it descends into flames.

This is a closed post. Only staff members may reply to closed posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This wasn't an intelligent comment. Spare me your smug arrogant schtick. Just because you believe all the anti-communist bullshit you've been fed your own life doesn't mean this is a bad article.

Capitalism is the most morally bankrupt, murderous ideology in history. Where is the morality in people starving in the streets because they didn't work hard enough? Where is the morality in polluting the Earth to make money?

And to say this is a pamphlet is ridiculous. Even Carlos Garcia is unwilling to believe Cartwright is evil. Don't let your stupid political hangups affect the way you ingest art, dude.

In the future, don't comment on my works if you're gonna say such stupid bullshit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So ignoring your adolescent comprehension of politics, morality and artistic interpretation for a moment, I'm curious why you think this takes any particular line on political matters.

The article itself is certainly sympathetic to the left but it doesn't take any particular moral line; the correctness of Ms. Zapatista's actions are criticised, quite legitimately, from multiple sources. Nobody here is turned into a strawman, and it's far more concerned with examining the varied reactions to Ms. Zapatista and her ideological approach than with taking sides.

And, even if it was, I think calling it "pamphletary" is absurd- it's hardly akin to when it's examining the nuances in the character's personalities and responses to a particular ideology in detail. If you're unable to keep your politics out of your artistic appreciation, that's your own business (and I suppose something we're all guilty of to an extent) but at least be accurate in your criticisms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well-crafted, for being pamphletary tripe of the most morally bankrupt, murderous ideology in history. The stink of totalitarianism and conscience-policing overtake the piece, so at least it could be argued that it works as meta-commentary on the entity, I guess.

It's well-crafted, but it can only operate under extremely flawed understandings of humanity and society that the reader must concede for it to work as intended (so the more knowledgeable the reader, the worse the piece).

It's also, frankly, morally appalling—buuuut, that's neither here nor there.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sic semper tyrannis.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was never the implication. The implication is that some humans support communism and are too afraid/lazy/accommodated to act. Nowhere does it say every human is subject to this. Please read more carefully next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The heavy-handed "real world" politics involved and the implication that the humans, by nature, support communism but are too afraid/lazy/accommodated to act (and Mrs. Zapatista just let their "fire" out) ruined this skip for me. In my opinion, it's more a well-written (really, the writing is very good, looking from a technical point of view) piece of political propaganda than an SCP, so that's a downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love her, I love her doggo headed bff (is it romantic? Idk), it's real interesting to me that this is set in the future (though it didnt seem like Mr. Destiny was set in anything but the present?)

But yeah this is good stuff and what I'm here for. I like the idea that GAW can have a bigger effect on the populace as time goes on and they escalate

Also I just love Jude okay

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but I feel like "Ms. Zapatista" comes across as more of a supervillain/antihero than an anomalous being, and that the article seems a bit too focused on generic "communism" as the theme rather than anything specific to the Zapatistas or Chiapas.

item.title: Re: There are a few things I'm unsure about.
item.content:encoded:
1. I have the rainbow logo because I fucking want to.
2. That's not at all what I'm saying. I'm not saying everyone is a communist. I'm saying some people sometimes don't act on their beliefs. Ms Zapatista helps them do that.
3. Bluntfiend was lying in that first phone call. You're misreading intent. He makes it clear in the second letter that the phone call was a lie to get Ms Zapatista in containment. Please read more carefully when you're going to fully dissect a text like this.
4. Yes, the Foundation is trying to protect capitalism more than normalcy. Because capitalism is normalcy.
5. I'm well-aware Ms Zapatista's powers are a Foundation no-no. It's why they're containing her. My point is maybe what she's doing isn't that bad. And it's a conclusion the O5 in the article obviously comes to.

And I just don't understand why an AI and an O5 having a friendly sort of banter is weird for you. There's nothing more to it. The AI is nice. And the O5 is nice.

And why is it "obviously problematic" for me to have real world politics in an article? We had a major site schism because of that. If you don't want any politics ever in these kind of deals, there are writing sites specifically made for that.

item.title: There are a few things I'm unsure about.
item.content:encoded:
First, why is the SCP logo a rainbow on this page and none of the others?

Secondly, What's the deal with the
"After while crocodile"
and
"That's okay!"
and the other oddly informal things that O5-6 sees when logging on and off? Is that supposed to be an A.I., an actual lover, or SCP-4669 herself talking? Is 05-6 infected?

+ show block
– hide block

Why are all the O-5 votes straight up almost unanimous? That seems a bit weird to me.

Third (fourth?), and I'm not entirely confident about this, but the article seems to be implying (corroborated by other commenters' interpretations) that normal baseline humanity supports communism and Ms. Zapatista is only freeing/inciting them so they can act. Beyond the obviously problematic insertion of real-world politics here, the Foundation is protrayed as wrong or evil due to trying to stop SCP-4669's anomalously powered intentions.

Regardless of whether or not baseline humanity in the Foundationverse supports communism (and therefore whether or not she is truly using mind-control), Ms. Zapatista is still in the wrong from a "secure/contain/protect" point of view because she is anomalously interfering when she anomalously inspires people to act on their beliefs.

I mean bluntfiend straight up tells the reader that Ms. Zapatista has anomalously caused him to act when previously it is implied he was somewhat opposed to the type of action she was taking.

"Ending capitalism is worthwhile, but not like this. Not mean."

And then:

"And if one hundred rich men have to die for this to happen? I won't mourn.

Ms. Zapatista has sparked the fire inside of me. Maybe it's time to let it out. We'll see."

Even though bluntfiend already had anti-capitalistic views, Ms. Zapatista anomalously changes the course of history by getting bluntfiend (and others) to act. Obviously to the foundation this is a big no-no.

So why is the O5 Council sweeping this under the rug?

(After Dr. Cartwright's friend dies)
"In a further decision (12-1), Dr. Cartwright was administered Class-C amnestics in order to expunge the interview from his memory, and the interview was marked for full expungement."

Is it to prevent the Dr. from grieving? Grief and amnestics have been adressed elsewhere (on both sides), but this is never really addressed in the article, and it seems to me as if the implication is that the Foundation is in fact somehow attempting to defend capitalism moreso than normalcy, but that just doesn't make any sense.

Gonna need a -EX on this one.

(Also sorry for mangling Ms. Zappo-Pasta's name.)


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn
(Edit by comment author: Oof, sorry. Thanks for telling me.)

Edit: Also I'm not exactly sure how replies and the forums work yet so it may be a while before I respond. Sorry in advance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda feel like this one would be stronger if Ms Zapatista didn't have 'aleakinetic' abilities. Just two important aspects:
1. The ability and will to make plans and carry them out, no matter how ruthless.
2. A voice that can't be forgotten and that forces the listener to comprehend and think through what she's saying.

I like it though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the ruling class will never willingly relinquish that power. upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you and I must have very different definitions of “fall from grace” because this seems neither horrific nor out of character for them

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interviews are kinda prosey for my taste. People are a little too good at wordplay and cracking wise, sort of reminds me of the silly interview log from 106-ARC.

Re: the narrative, SCP-4669 seems way OP and doesn't fit the established Misters Against Weed M.O.—the original Little Misters all feel neatly interrelated, like siblings, whereas I can't imagine SCP-4669 having anything to do with Mr. Meme or Mr. Deadly Sins. There's just too much tonal variance in this family and I don't like it.

I describe myself as liking GAW, but I think my biggest issue w/ them is the constant tone shift between light silly articles about left-leaning internet culture, and heavy-handed epics about death and intrigue that are borderline tale-worthy. I prefer a GOI where I can open an article knowing "oh, this is a Wondertainment article", or something, and know for mostly-certain what kind of article I'm gonna read. I just don't like articles structured like this.

And I know there is no canon, every article is its own universe/timeline, yadda yadda, but it's kinda ballsy to a god-modding degree to forecast GAW's fall from grace, like, ten years from present day. That level of certainty's kind of offputting.

item.title: Whoop, whoop
item.content:encoded: "Sound of the Police" is easily my all-time favorite MTF name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: does she wear one of those balaclavas tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed that you upvoted this article's 'brother' (we posted them at the same time because it was cool), Mr. Destiny. I wrote that and I want to say that clearly right now that with the way they function, Mr. Destiny is actually more mind affecting than Ms. Zapatista. Zapatista, like, prevents mind effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The angle taken there is that SCP-4669 doesn't brainwash anyone, and the Foundation steadfastly believes that it has to be brainwashing and the like, because there's no other explanation for it in their view. It contrasts with what's actually happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not at all what's happening here. Did you read this carefully?

Being uncontainable due to the power of plot is one hundred percent dead on though. That's definitely an issue. I could've gone with something stronger than her infantry just manifesting inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a mind-bender scip who anomalously converts people to communism via vocal interaction and cannot be contained because she -literally- has the power of plot, and the only people immune to her magic are that way because they've got no empathy for their fellow humans.
…
This is excellently written but the subtext is morally appalling, "it's ok to brainwash people, because if they really care about other people they don't really mind."
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would agree, but it seems like Eskobar enjoyed it as his first GAW scp. So I'm unsure what move to make on that.

Also I don't think it'd be dated when he's alive in 2027 because, well, this isn't real. In GAW world, Elon Musk dies then. It's okay if he doesn't in ours. Or even dies before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't feel like this stands on its own; you need to know other GAW skips to understand it, or at least get the full effect. Also not a fan of including real people in skips; kind of tired of beating that drum, actually. :/ This is gonna look really dated when Elon Musk is still alive in 2027.

SCP-4669 is an Epsilon-Level priority.

Ignoring that Epsilon-Level doesn't sound like a very high priority, I think this is missing something. Containment, termination, neutralization, what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always jealous of people who have skill in storycrafting. The pacing has such a dark, ominous feel that seems effortless. I can imagine the scenes in this taking place in muted colors, almost black-and-white, like an arthouse film. I'm told this isn't what most GAW articles are like, but if this were the only GAW that existed, I would want to see a lot more of it. +1 and whatnot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always happy to help my friends. :) Here's to another excellent article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing to watch this grow! Congrats on another great Mister! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks to uncle nicolini (crit AND css!), communismwillwin, decibelles, scarhaver, a random day, tiefling, cuppabro, filayoung, secretcrow, petrograd, the great hippo, dog teeth, djkaktus! (who was going to be my cowriter before i went and wrote the whole scp myself), and anyone else i forgot.
